findings.message: The new `typestate_names`-driven outgoing computation is not covered by any test in this file: every updated test still passes `HashSet::new()`, so a regression in the intersection logic or `set_outgoing()` path would slip through. This public API now has an untested behavior branch.

No test covers the new `typestate_names` filtering path. Every existing test still calls `build_type_graph(..., &HashSet::new())`, so the outgoing-transition logic at this point can regress without any assertion failure. Please add a case that supplies typestate names and verifies `TypeNode::outgoing()` only includes the filtered typestate targets.

`build_type_graph` still resolves collisions by plain type name and keeps the last entry it sees. Because `SchemaExport` only sorts by `name`, equal-name types retain the original `krate.index` hash iteration order, so the winner is nondeterministic. This repo already has two public `Verdict` enums (`review::Verdict` and `review_v2::Verdict`), which means the new collision path can flip between runs and produce unstable `TypeGraph` contents/module paths.

The reverse-direction check is underspecified: the ADR/metadata require `ConsistencyReport` to track `undeclared_traits`, but the spec only requires detecting undeclared types. That lets an implementation ignore missing trait declarations and still pass review, which weakens the CI guardrail the track is adding.

T001's verification only checks a single `done` track's `spec.md`. It does not exercise an archived track or the other rendered views (`plan.md` / `domain-types.md`), so the manual verification plan cannot catch the regression this task is meant to prevent.

T005 only verifies that undeclared types are detected, but the ADR's reverse-check contract also requires undeclared traits to be reported in `ConsistencyReport`. As written, the spec can pass while trait drift remains untested.

The combined `T005-T006` verification step checks only undeclared-type detection. It never validates that `sotp verify spec-code-consistency` emits the `ConsistencyReport` output required by T006, so the CLI contract can regress without this checklist catching it.

T001's acceptance criteria only assert that `track-sync-views` leaves done/archived track views untouched. The ADR and metadata make `registry.md` regeneration part of the same fix, but there is no success criterion for that behavior, so an implementation that stops updating `registry.md` would still satisfy the current spec.

T004 requires both capturing `module_path` and warning on same-name type collisions, but the acceptance criteria only cover `module_path` extraction. A change that stores module paths yet never emits the collision warning would still pass the track's stated success conditions.

T004's only acceptance criterion covers `module_path` extraction, but the task/ADR also require emitting a warning on same-name collisions. As written, an implementation could skip the `tracing::warn` path entirely and still satisfy the spec's done criteria.

The T005/T006 acceptance criteria verify reverse detection and report emission, but they never require `forward_signals` to be populated correctly from the existing spec->code evaluation. That leaves a reverse-only implementation able to pass this track even though the goal and ADR both call for bidirectional consistency checking.

`cargo make ci` is an acceptance criterion for every task, but the verification checklist never asks the implementer to run it. Someone can complete every listed verification step without exercising the actual repository gate.

T001 is underspecified in the success criteria. The ADR and metadata make this fix responsible for still regenerating `registry.md` while skipping done/archived track views, but the only acceptance criterion checks the non-overwrite side. An implementation that stops updating `registry.md` entirely would still satisfy the current spec.

T002's contract says `collect_type_names` must keep unwrapping both `Result<T, E>` and `Option<T>`, but the acceptance criteria only exercise the `Vec<T>` negative case and the `Result` positive case. A change that accidentally stops unwrapping `Option<T>` would still pass this track as written.

The T003 verification step asks for `grep -r CodeProfile` over the whole codebase, but this repository intentionally contains historical docs and ADRs that must continue to mention `CodeProfile`. Even after the implementation is correct, this checklist item will still fail on unrelated documentation, so it cannot reliably verify the rename.

`T004`'s written acceptance contract dropped the `TypeInfo.module_path` requirement even though both the task scope and ADR require adding `module_path` to `TypeInfo` and `TypeNode`. As written, an implementation that only populates `TypeNode.module_path` would satisfy the track while still violating the planned rustdoc-to-domain data flow.

`verification.md` never checks the main behavioral change for `T003`: that `TypeGraph.outgoing` is filtered down to typestate transitions only. The only `T003` manual step is a `CodeProfile` grep, so this track can be marked verified without exercising the new transition-filtering behavior called out in the ADR and acceptance criteria.

`verification.md` checks that done/archived track views stay untouched after `cargo make track-sync-views`, but it never verifies the paired T001 acceptance criterion that `registry.md` is still regenerated. A regression that skips registry updates entirely would still satisfy this checklist.

The T004 verification only asks for a same-name-type warning, while `spec.json` also requires proving that `TypeInfo.module_path` is extracted from rustdoc JSON and propagated into `TypeNode.module_path`. As written, the checklist would miss an implementation that emits a warning without actually wiring the `module_path` data flow correctly.

The T006 step only checks that the CLI output contains `forward_signals`, `undeclared_types`, and `undeclared_traits`; it does not verify that `forward_signals` is actually the correct result of `evaluate_domain_type_signals` as required by the acceptance criteria. A broken forward-direction consistency report could therefore pass this verification plan.

`T005` is the only task referenced for the reverse-direction check, but its description only covers undeclared `TypeGraph` nodes and never mentions trait reporting. Since `spec.json` assigns `undeclared_traits` to `T005`, the plan can be marked complete without implementing half of ADR Decision 4.

The T002 checklist only verifies the negative case (`Vec<T>` must not expose `T` in `return_type_names`). It never checks the required positive unwrap behavior for `Option<T>`/`Result<T, E>`, so a regression in the new selective-unwrapping logic would still pass verification.

This verification step compares `ConsistencyReport.forward_signals` against `sotp track signals`, but that command evaluates spec source-tag signals, not domain-type signals. As written, the checklist cannot validate whether the new forward consistency output matches `evaluate_domain_type_signals`.

The track introduces a new public CLI surface (`sotp verify spec-code-consistency`), but the acceptance contract only covers the happy path of emitting a `ConsistencyReport`. AGENTS.md requires happy-path and error-case tests for public APIs, and there is no requirement here to verify fail-closed behavior for invalid track IDs, unreadable `domain-types.json`, or schema/export failures. As written, incomplete error propagation could ship while still satisfying the track.

T005's manual verification invokes `sotp verify spec-code-consistency` without the `--track-id` and `--crate` arguments that the spec requires for this CLI, so the checklist does not actually exercise the documented command contract.

T002's verification only checks `Vec<T>` non-expansion and `Option<T>` expansion; it never exercises the `Result<T, E>` path that the spec explicitly relies on. If `collect_type_names()` regresses on `Result`, this checklist would still pass.

T006 is missing any error-path verification for invalid `track-id` or unreadable `domain-types.json`, even though the spec requires a non-zero exit code and clear error message for those cases. That leaves the CLI's failure handling untested.

T002 is underspecified: the ADR’s Decision 1 requires `collect_type_names` to keep unwrapping `BorrowedRef` and to stop recursing into tuples, but this spec/verification only assert the Vec/Result/Option cases. An implementation could satisfy the current checklist and still violate the intended `collect_type_names` behavior for borrowed references or tuples.

T002 is under-tested as written: the checklist only exercises `Vec<T>` for the non-expanding generic-wrapper case, but the task description explicitly also forbids recursion into other wrappers like `HashMap` and `Box`. A regression that still unwraps those wrappers could pass this verification plan.

T001 is underspecified: the acceptance criteria only require preserving done/archived views and still regenerating `registry.md`, but never assert that active track views continue to be regenerated. An implementation that stops rendering all per-track views and only updates `registry.md` would still satisfy the current spec while breaking `track-sync-views` for planned/in-progress tracks.

T003's in-scope requirement drops the ADR's requirement that typestate filtering move into TypeGraph construction (`build_type_graph`). With only the rename and `outgoing` field called out here, an implementation could keep filtering in `evaluate_domain_type_signals` and still satisfy the written spec, which misses the planned architecture change.

T002's acceptance criteria cover `Vec<T>` and `Option<T>` only, even though the in-scope requirement also mandates preserving `BorrowedRef` unwrapping and removing tuple expansion. A regression in `&T` or tuple handling would still satisfy every listed acceptance criterion.

`S1` says the fix may be implemented by filtering `collect_track_snapshots()` by status, but `sync_rendered_views()` uses that same snapshot list to render `registry.md`. Taking this option would drop `done`/`archived` tracks from the registry entirely, even though the intended change is only to stop rewriting their per-track views.

`T005` promises that reverse checking will detect every undeclared type/trait, but the spec still keeps `domain-types.json` keyed by bare name and `T004` only warns on same-name collisions. In the motivating `review::Error` vs `guard::Error` case, a single declared `Error` entry would mask the other code type, so this design can still produce false negatives.

T002's acceptance contract only exercises `Vec<Published>` for the 'do not recurse into generic wrappers' rule, even though the task explicitly forbids unwrapping other wrappers such as `HashMap`, `Box`, and `Arc`. An implementation that still expands those wrappers would satisfy every written acceptance criterion here.

This T006 verification step cannot prove `forward_signals` is correct: `sotp track domain-type-signals` only emits a blue/red summary and writes signals back to `domain-types.json`, so comparing against the command's 'output' does not validate the per-entry `evaluate_domain_type_signals` result. A broken `ConsistencyReport.forward_signals` could still pass as long as aggregate counts match.

T001 only defines success around the bulk `cargo make track-sync-views` path, but the same overwrite bug can still occur through `sync_rendered_views(..., Some(track_id))`, which is what `track transition`/state operations call after a track becomes `done`. As written, the plan can pass its acceptance checks while still regenerating a just-completed legacy track's views on status transition.

`Scope Verified` requires every ADR decision section to be covered by this track, but ADR 2026-04-08-0045 Decision 5 explicitly defers Yellow reintroduction to a future track. As written, the checklist is impossible to satisfy without scope creep or a false sign-off.

The T001 single-track verification only checks a `done` track. The ADR and acceptance criteria require both `done` and `archived` tracks to be skipped, so an `archived`-only regression in the `track_id=Some(...)` path would pass this checklist.

The change marks `T002` as `done`, but the review brief and changed-file description both state this update was supposed to complete `T001`. As written, the source-of-truth metadata advances the wrong task and leaves the intended task-state update unreviewed.

The change marks `T003` as `done`, but this review batch is supposed to record completion of `T002`. Because `plan.md` is generated from `metadata.json`, both files now consistently report the wrong task as completed, which makes the track state inaccurate.

`updated_at` is still earlier than `created_at` (`2026-04-08T09:02:37Z` vs `2026-04-08T14:00:00Z`), so this metadata now records an impossible timeline and can misorder or invalidate consumers that rely on timestamp monotonicity.

`plan.md` still marks T006 as in progress (`[~]`) even though `metadata.json` marks the same task `done`. Because `plan.md` is the generated view, this leaves the rendered track out of sync with the source of truth and makes the track look unfinished.

`plan.md` still renders T006 as in-progress (`[~]`) even though `metadata.json` marks the same task as `done`. Since this file is declared as a generated view from `metadata.json`, the two files are now self-contradictory and the rendered plan will misrepresent the track's completion state.

`TypeNode::new` now creates nodes with `outgoing` always empty, while `evaluate_typestate` reads only `outgoing()` and ignores `method_return_types`. Any caller that constructs a `TypeGraph` directly and forgets the extra `set_outgoing()` step will get false missing-transition reds, so the new public API no longer makes this invariant self-consistent or hard to misuse.

`TypeNode::new()` now always initializes `outgoing` as empty, while `evaluate_typestate()` consumes only `outgoing()` and no longer falls back to `method_return_types()`. Because `TypeGraph`/`TypeNode` are still public and re-exported, any caller that constructs a graph directly through this API will silently get false-red typestate evaluations unless they know to call `set_outgoing()` themselves. That hidden invariant was not present before this rename and is not enforced by the type system.

`TypeNode::new()` now always builds a node with `outgoing` empty, while typestate evaluation reads only `outgoing()` and no longer falls back to `method_return_types()`. Because `TypeNode`/`TypeGraph` are still public and re-exported, any caller that constructs a graph directly with `TypeNode::new` will silently get false-red missing-transition results unless they remember a separate `set_outgoing()` call. This introduces a new hidden invariant into the domain API instead of making the invalid state unrepresentable.

`TypeNode::new()` now always initializes `outgoing` as empty, while typestate evaluation reads only `outgoing()` and no longer falls back to `method_return_types()`. Because `TypeNode`/`TypeGraph` remain public (and are re-exported), any caller that constructs a graph with `TypeNode::new()` will silently get false-red missing-transition results unless they know to make a separate `set_outgoing()` call. That introduces a new hidden invariant into the library API.

The updated typestate tests still do not exercise the new `outgoing` behavior. `make_profile_with_transition()` sets `method_return_types` and `outgoing` to the same value, so this suite cannot catch regressions where evaluation accidentally consults `method_return_types()` again or where non-typestate return types leak into `outgoing`. Add a case where the two sets differ and assert that only `outgoing` drives the result.

`TypeNode::new()` now always initializes `outgoing` as empty, while typestate evaluation consumes only `outgoing()` and no longer falls back to `method_return_types()`. Because `TypeNode`/`TypeGraph` are public and re-exported, any caller that constructs a graph directly with `TypeNode::new()` will silently get false-red missing-transition results unless they remember a separate `set_outgoing()` call. This introduces a new hidden invalid state into the public domain API instead of making the invariant self-enforcing.

`TypeNode::new` now takes `outgoing` as an unconstrained public input, while `evaluate_typestate` consumes only `outgoing()` as the source of typestate transitions. That means any direct `TypeGraph` construction can omit or invent transitions and silently produce false red/blue results, because the required `outgoing ⊆ method_return_types` invariant is no longer enforced by the domain API.

`TypeNode::new` still accepts an arbitrary `outgoing` subset even though `evaluate_typestate` now ignores `method_return_types()` and trusts only `outgoing()`. Because `TypeNode`/`TypeGraph` remain public and are re-exported, any direct construction with `method_return_types = {"Published"}` and `outgoing = {}` will silently produce a false-red missing-transition result. This introduces a new hidden invariant into the public domain API instead of making the typestate edge set derivable or validated.

New public module-path APIs were added here (`TypeInfo::with_module_path`/`module_path` and `TypeNode::set_module_path`/`module_path`), but this file's tests still cover only the pre-existing constructors/accessors. That leaves the new `Some(...)` path and mutation path unverified, which is a P1 gap under this repo's testing policy for public APIs.

The new `check_consistency` tests use `HashMap::new()` without importing `HashMap`, so this module's test build will fail before the added coverage can run. Bring `std::collections::HashMap` into scope in the test module.

The new typestate-name wiring in `execute_domain_type_signals` is not exercised by any test in this file. The existing tests only cover read/parse failures plus an ignored end-to-end happy path, so a regression in the new `typestate_names` collection or `build_type_graph(&schema, &typestate_names)` call would slip through unnoticed. Add a focused test that uses a non-empty typestate set and verifies the filtered outgoing transitions.

`SpecCodeConsistencyArgs` will parse `--crate-name` by default, but the documented CLI contract here and in the ADR is `--crate <name>`. As written, the expected flag is rejected and the new subcommand cannot be invoked as specified.

The stdout payload is not a `ConsistencyReport` JSON object. It emits `forward_signals_count` plus debug-formatted slices, so the required `forward_signals` field is missing and the output is not guaranteed to be valid JSON for arbitrary identifiers. That breaks the documented contract for this subcommand.

The stdout JSON omits `found_items`, `missing_items`, and `extra_items` from each `forward_signals` entry, so this command does not emit the full `ConsistencyReport` shape required by T006 and callers cannot verify per-entry signal details.

There are no tests for the new `SpecCodeConsistency` path, so the invalid-track-id, unreadable `domain-types.json`, and schema-export failure branches are unguarded despite this being a new public CLI surface.

The new report payload is hand-built with `format!`/`println!`, so any quote, backslash, or newline in a type or trait name will emit invalid JSON. `DomainTypeEntry::new` only rejects empty names, so this path is reachable with valid input. Serialize a struct with `serde_json` instead of concatenating strings.

`execute()` always calls `print_outcome(label, &outcome)` after printing the JSON blob, so stdout is no longer a pure `ConsistencyReport` stream. That breaks the new command’s machine-readable contract and makes downstream parsing fragile. Either suppress the human summary for `spec-code-consistency` or send it to stderr.

The new subcommand prints the JSON report and then `print_outcome()` appends the normal human-readable verify footer, so stdout is not a standalone `ConsistencyReport` payload. The acceptance criteria say this command should emit the report itself.

The emitted JSON is not a full `ConsistencyReport`: each `forward_signals` entry is reduced to `{type_name, kind_tag, signal}` and drops `found_type`, `found_items`, `missing_items`, and `extra_items`. Callers cannot reconstruct the per-entry mismatch details the report is supposed to carry.

`spec-code-consistency` no longer emits a machine-readable `ConsistencyReport`: the `forward_signals` JSON here only includes `type_name`, `kind_tag`, and `signal`, dropping `found_type`, `found_items`, `missing_items`, and `extra_items`, and `execute()` still passes the result through `print_outcome`, which appends human banners after the JSON blob. That breaks the stdout contract expected by T006 and any downstream parser.

`matches!(e, SchemaExportError::NightlyNotFound)` moves `e`, so the later `format!("schema export failed: {e}{hint}")` is a use-after-move and this arm will not compile. Borrow the error in the predicate instead.

`spec-code-consistency` is supposed to emit a standalone `ConsistencyReport`, but this path prints a hand-built JSON fragment and then `execute()` still calls `print_outcome()`, so stdout is no longer machine-readable. The JSON also drops `found_type`, `found_items`, `missing_items`, and `extra_items` from each `forward_signals` entry.
This new public CLI subcommand has no happy-path or error-path tests in the module, so regressions in track-id validation, schema export failures, or the stdout contract can ship unnoticed.

`matches!(e, SchemaExportError::NightlyNotFound)` moves `e`, so the later `format!("schema export failed: {e}{hint}")` uses a moved value and this branch will not compile. Borrow the error in the predicate (`matches!(&e, ...)`) or match on `&e` instead.

The JSON line is assembled with manual `format!("\"{s}\"")` interpolation and similar string concatenation, but none of the values are escaped. Because `DomainTypeEntry::name()` accepts arbitrary non-empty strings, a quote, backslash, or newline in a type/item name will emit invalid JSON. Use a real serializer such as `serde_json::json!`/`to_string` or escape the strings before printing.

`execute()` still calls `print_outcome()` after emitting the JSON report, so stdout is a JSON blob plus human banners/findings. That violates the new command’s `ConsistencyReport` stdout contract and will break any parser expecting pure JSON.

There are no tests covering the new `SpecCodeConsistency` path. The new happy path plus invalid-track-ID, unreadable `domain-types.json`, and schema-export failure branches can regress without detection.
